metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: Map of Italy from Ptolemy's Cosmographia (Ulm, 1482)

metadata.label: Format
metadata.value: 1 map : woodcut, hand color ; 35 x 54 cm

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: The woodcuts in the Ulm Ptolemy are generally credited to Johannes 'Schnitzer' ("the woodblock-cutter") from Armsheim. One characteristic of his work is that he tended to reverse his capital 'N's when cutting the letter. Of the Italian editions of Ptolemy only one, that of Bernard Sylvanus, published in Venice in 1511, used woodcuts. All the others used copper engravings.

metadata.value: The North part of America conteyning Newfoundland, New England, Virginia, Florida, New Spaine and Nova Francia, with ye riche Iles of Hispaniola, Cuba, Jamaica, and Porto Rieco, on the south, and upon ye west the large and goodly island of California

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: The first printed map in English to show California as an island, with flat northern coast.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Cities, etc. ; shoals; pictorial symbols; notes.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Notes on map refer to California, Sr. Thomas Button and Mr. Baffin.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: "852" & "853" inside neat line in top left & right corners of map.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Engraved by: R. Elstrake Sculpsit (lower right).

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: On the map are two notes, one stating"California sometymes supposed to be a part of ye westerne continent, but scince by a Spanish Charte taken by ye Hollanders it is found to be a goodly Ilande: the length of the west shoare beeing about 500 leagues..."

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Issued in: Hakluytus Posthumus or Purchas His Pilgrimes, III, 852-3. London, 1625, to accompany an article by H. Briggs on the Northwest Passage.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: The Glen McLaughlin Map Collection of California as an Island

items.label: Composite: 48 Globe Gores, 2 Polar Calottes, Celestial Globe

canvases.label: Composite: 48 Globe Gores, 2 Polar Calottes, Celestial Globe

metadata.label: Short Title
metadata.value: Composite: 48 Globe Gores, 2 Polar Calottes, Celestial Globe

metadata.label: Full Title
metadata.value: (Composite) 48 Globe Gores, 2 Polar Calottes, Celestial Globe)

metadata.label: Pub Title
metadata.value: [Complete set of counterproof plates of globe gores and calottes for a 3.5-foot diameter celestial globe]

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: "Exceedingly rare complete set of the counter-proof version of the engraved gores for CoronelliÔÇÖs 3.5-foot celestial globe. This issue is before the addition of the engraved captions and figures; it was intended to show the heavens as they would appear to an observer on earth, as opposed to the conventional celestial globe that represents the stars as seen on an imaginary sphere from the outside. This copy is in exceptional original condition: a set of untrimmed sheets in their original binding, exactly as they would be received by a globe maker. On 7 December 1692 Sr Carlo Malavista, in a lecture on the development of astronomy at the Accademia Fisico-Matematica in Rome, described CoronelliÔÇÖs globes as the most perfect yet produced. He ends his peroration with remarks on the difficulty of using a celestial globe. ÔÇťBoth these globes give to the eye the appearance of being equal, but they are not equally useful to the eyes of the mind, that is, the intelligence, because to express the earth on a globe can be very well done, because the earth is in the shape of a ball on which we walk. The sky on the other hand is the opposite of this. As we stand on the earth the sky is above us, and we observe it as concave and not convex.ÔÇŁ It was the accepted convention, as Malavista pointed out, to depict the heavens on a celestial globe as they would appear to an observer beyond the heavens. Malavista continued: ÔÇťTo make it easier to understand, the illustrious Giovanni Ciampini, Director of the Academy, has thought fit to have the celestial globe of Coronelli divided into two halves in concave form, so that the stars are seen in their proper location, agreeing with the way we look at them in the sky...ÔÇŁ This is the first record in print of CoronelliÔÇÖs concave globes. When Coronelli issued in 1693 a new edition of the celestial globe with fewer names of constellations, he put the proposed innovation into practice by producing the globe in two versions, convex and concave. In CoronelliÔÇÖs account of his travels of 1696-97,┬áViaggi dÔÇÖItalia in Inghilterra┬á(1697), a ÔÇśNotice to the Public on the globes of Father CoronelliÔÇÖ is appended to vol. II (pp. 204-5). This reports that the celestial globes have been printed, some in convex forms, some in concave form, and that they have been made in this way, not through the mistake of the author, as some so knowledgeable people thought, but for their greater usefulness. The Accademia degli Argonauti made the same point in 1704: ÔÇťcritics construed as a mistakeÔÇŁ what was done with the greatest erudition and for the use of our astronomers, namely that our Cosmographer engraved the celestial globe in concave and convex...ÔÇŁ A mounted example of the gores for the ÔÇťconcaveÔÇŁ globe is recorded in the Bayerisches NationalmuseumÔÇÖ (Helen Wallis, preface to the facsimile edition of the┬áLibro dei globi Venice 1693 (1701), Amsterdam 1(1969) p. ix.┬á which omits 2 of the plates missing from the BL example reproduced here) This ÔÇśconcaveÔÇÖ version, as distinct from the more standard ÔÇśconvex version,ÔÇÖ was produced by counter-proofing the original plates, not by re-engraving them: A strongly inked impression of the ÔÇśconvex versionÔÇÖ would be made on a sheet of impermeable paper, and then a second impression immediately made from that sheet while the ink was still wet, so that the plate image is reversed. The final two plates in our copy are not part of the┬áLibro dei globi┬ábut are added and appear to be pattern plates for globe makers. Father Vincenzo Coronelli (1650-1718) was a Minorite friar who was an exceptional cartographer and globe maker. His first pair of globes were made for the Duke of Parma, and this led to his being commissioned to create a pair of globes for Louis XIV. They were completed after two yearsÔÇÖ work in 1683, and were the largest globes ever made until the 1920s; they were 3.87-meters in diameter and each weighed two tons (they are now in the Biblioth├Ęque nationale in Paris). Upon his return to Venice in 1684 he founded EuropeÔÇÖs first geographical society, the Accademia Cosmografica degli Argonauti. He was appointed Cosmographer of Venice, and produced a series of globes, atlases, and maps notable for their accuracy and detail." (Martayan Lan, W.P. Watson, 2020). Georeferencing of globe gores done by Cartography Associates in 2021. Gores georeferenced in Polyconic projection, then converted to Geographic, and other projections.  "Mostly celestial globes show the starry sky as it would appear to an observer from space.┬áThe constellations can then all be seen in mirror image, so that orientation is difficult for the viewer.┬áVincenzo Coronelli, on the other hand, reproduces the constellations on this globe as they appear from the earth and therefore in the perspective we are familiar with." (catalog Bayerisches Nationalmuseum). Interestingly, gore 42 is not reversed - the counter-proof gore was never made, yet the gore does not have any text applied and it is a very dark impression so we must assume that it was ready to make the counter-proof but for some reason it was not done. For our digital version we have reversed the plate image to create our own digital counter-proof.

metadata.label: Full Title
metadata.value: Haikou Quantu ŠÁĚňĆúňůĘňťľ [Complete Map of Sea Ports]. Wanguo tu & Wukou Yanhai Shuilu Quantu ŔÉČňťőňťľ & ń║öňĆúŠ▓┐ŠÁĚŠ░┤ŔĚ»ňůĘňťľ [Map of 10,000 Countries & Complete Map of the Five Coastal Estuaries].

metadata.label: Pub Title
metadata.value: Haikou Quantu ŠÁĚňĆúňůĘňťľ [Complete Map of Sea Ports]. Wanguo tu & Wukou Yanhai Shuilu Quantu ŔÉČňťőňťľ & ń║öňĆúŠ▓┐ŠÁĚŠ░┤ŔĚ»ňůĘňťľ [Map of 10,000 Countries & Complete Map of the Five Coastal Estuaries].

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: "A fine hand-coloured manuscript map painted on paper with numerous annotations in Chinese. Map measuring 665x30cm, mounted on paper as a handscroll, empty pink mica-flecked frontispiece (detached), housed in a 20th century wooden box. Occasional light creasing, a few very minor marginal tears, but otherwise in very good condition. Some English annotation written in pencil. Three red seals of the map production supervisor (Šľ░ńŞëšŤŐŔÖčšŤúŔúŻ) along the upper edge. N.p., n.d. [but early 19th century]. This rare manuscript military map follows the pattern established during the 18th century: It opens with a map of the eastern hemisphere, showing Europe incl. Germany (Huangqi Ú╗äšąü), Denmark (LinyinňÉŁňŤá - the stingy one!), Holland (Helan ŔŹĚňů░), France (Folangxi ńŻŤňů░Ŕą┐), England (Yingjili Ŕő▒Šť║Ú╗Ä) & Portugal (Putaoya ŔĹíŔÉäšëÖ), Africa, Arabia, India, Russia, China, Japan, and South East Asia. Further south is Australia (Dashishan ňĄžšč│ň▒▒, Great Stone Mountain) with a note on the left stating that ÔÇťon the northern shore of this place ÔÇśGreat Stone MountainÔÇÖ you can anchor boatsÔÇŁ (ŠşĄŔÖĽňĄž šč│ň▒▒ňÉĹňîŚňíóňůžňĆ»Š│ŐŔł╣) and to its right is another un-named continent (ÔÇťno traces of humans can be foundÔÇŁ ń║║Ŕ┐╣ńŞŹňł░ŔÖĽ). Of the Arctic Ocean, it is said: ÔÇťFor six months in the year this sea is without water and for six months the day has no evening.ÔÇŁ. The concept of an eastern hemisphere is clearly based on Western cartography, however, the map as such is based on a Chinese Geography entitled Hai guo wen jian lu (ŠÁĚňťőŔü×ŔŽőÚîä) by Chen Lunjiong (?-1751, Admiral of the Guangdong Navy) first published in 1744. Throughout the 18th century Chen was instrumental in stepping up the construction of fortifications in strategically important locations along the coast and this map documents his lasting influence. The Eastern Hemisphere is followed by a long continuous coastal chart stretching from the Manchu capital Mukden (now Shenyang) down to Vietnam: It provides details of geographical features like mountains, islands, reefs, sandbanks, and harbours. It indicates the location of coastal towns and villages, defensive structures and numerous military garrisons (identified with a little red flag). The scroll takes you from Shengjing to Tianjin, across Shandong Peninsula with the capital Peking at the top, down the Yellow River estuary, the Yangtze River estuary, the Qiantang estuary, Ningbo (Zhejiang), Fujian, Taiwan, Guangdong, Macau, Hainan, Dongsha, Xisha, and Vietnam. Macao is given special prominence with three walls guarding the Portuguese settlement. At the very end of the scroll the following comment is written in the top right corner: ÔÇťSiam is an ancient red land, during the Ming dynasty Siam and the Lavo kingdom united as a dynasty and were given tribute status.ÔÇŁ (ŠÜ╣šżůňťőňŹ│ňĆĄňŁ┤ŠśÄňžĹ ňÉłŠÜ╣ŔłçšżůŠľŤšé║ńŞÇŠťČŠťŁňŤáń╣őňĆŚň░üŠťŁŔ▓ó). It is interesting to note that during the Qing dynasty Chinese Costal charts were not drawn to a specific scale. The further you move away from the capital the smaller the scale, i.e. the distance from Macao to Taiwan appears to be relatively longer than from Peking to Taiwan. Furthermore, the map does not follow a standardised grid system but simply follows the coastline regardless of whether it goes from North to South, or East to West. All the features and topographical details are rendered with precision and the characters are written in regular kaishu script used by official scribes. The outer green silk brocade was used for documents relating to naval defence during the Qing dynasty and thus the purpose of the map is of a military nature. This coastal chart is undated but due to the fact that character for ÔÇťningÔÇŁ (ň»ž part of the personal name of the Daoguang Emperor) is not a taboo, leads us to assume that the map was made during the Jiaqing period, i.e. before 1820. No mention is made of the English presence in Hong Kong." (Maggs Bros., 2022)

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: "The Best Russian Mapping of Northern California. Prepared at Sitka in 1848. This chart, created in 1848 by Mikhail Dmitrievich Tebenkov in Novo Arkhangel'sk (present-day Sitka, Alaska), delineates the coast of northern California and southern Oregon from Heceta Head in the north to roughly Fort Bragg in the south. The chart includes three coastal views alongside a detailed inset of Humboldt Bay. This example is from the collection of Warren Heckrotte and bears a pencil inscription by him highlighting it as "A well known chart, & historically significant, because of inset map of Humboldt Bay." During the mid-19th century, Russian presence in North America, particularly along the Pacific Northwest, was marked by extensive exploration and the establishment of trading posts and settlements, namely under the auspices of the Russian-American Company. The creation of this chart by Tebenkov, a notable figure in Russian maritime exploration, underscores the era's Russian efforts in charting and understanding these territories, reflecting the broader imperial ambitions and the scientific curiosity of the period. Tebenkov's work, especially his detailed attention to the coastal geography, contributed significantly to the navigational knowledge and the strategic understanding of this remote part of the world. This example's lack of folds, as compared to the atlas version published in Tebenkov's Atlas sieverozapadnykh beregov Ameriki in 1852, suggests that this chart may have been separately issued. Based on the working navigational and mathematical annotations on the map and in the margins, it appears that this example was used by a Russian vessel off the North American coast in the mid-19th century. Rarity OCLC identifies several copies of the atlas but finds just a single separate map at the University of California Berkeley, highlighting its lack of a plate number unlike the atlas versions. " (Ruderman, 2024)

metadata.label: Note
metadata.value: Close-up of sextant instrument itself. Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon. A standard brass and steel sextant, modified by George Davidson to include a spirit-level horizon for inland observations where no sea horizon was available. Likely made by Troughton & Simms, London, c. 1865.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope Owner/Exhibitor: George Davidson (1825ÔÇô1911), U.S. Coast Survey Maker: Sextant likely Troughton & Simms, London (frame style, c. 1860s) Modification: Spirit-level horizon attachment devised by Davidson (Journal of the Franklin Institute, 1866) Associated Instrument: Dipleidoscope by Elliott Bros., London, in fitted leather case Provenance: Exhibited at the Exposition Universelle, Paris, 1867 (Group 2, Class 12, No. 63) Description Sextant: Precision arc instrument, with telescope and mirrors, modified by Davidson to carry a spirit-level horizon for use where no sea horizon was visible. Engraved nameplate: ÔÇťGeorge Davidson, Philadelphia.ÔÇŁ Original wooden case with painted inscription recording its entry in the Paris Exposition of 1867. Dipleidoscope: Compact brass instrument with multiple glass prisms and attached telescope, inscribed ÔÇťElliott Bros., 449 West Strand, London.ÔÇŁ Contained in original velvet-lined morocco leather case. Historical Context The Exposition Universelle in Paris (AprilÔÇôNovember 1867) drew international exhibits of scientific and industrial progress. Group 2, Class 12 covered ÔÇťinstruments of precision.ÔÇŁ Davidson entered his modified sextant on behalf of the U.S. Coast Survey; official reports list ÔÇťDavidson, G., Washington; sextant.ÔÇŁ Jury accounts record that the instrument earned honorable mention. The sextant illustrates U.S. Coast Survey field adaptations of standard British instruments for rugged inland and coastal survey work. The accompanying dipleidoscope reflects DavidsonÔÇÖs broader toolkit: a portable device for determining true noon by merging solar images, allowing accurate chronometer checks without a full observatory transit. Subsequent History Case markings show later shipment: ÔÇťFrom T. D. Davidson, 1721 Washington St., S.F., Cal. ÔÇö To Hon. F. D. Roosevelt, Naval Observatory, Washington D.C.ÔÇŁ Roosevelt, Assistant Secretary of the Navy (1913ÔÇô1920), oversaw Naval Observatory instruments. The valuation was listed as $100. Significance Demonstrates DavidsonÔÇÖs role as both surveyor and instrument innovator. Connects the instrument to the Paris Exposition of 1867, a major stage for scientific and industrial display. Association with Franklin D. Roosevelt in his Navy Department role adds a later layer of provenance. The inclusion of the dipleidoscope reveals DavidsonÔÇÖs combined use of angular and temporal instruments to replicate observatory precision in the field. Exhibition History Paris Exposition, 1867, Group 2, Class 12, No. 63 (scientific instruments). Provenance Timeline c. 1865 ÔÇö Sextant manufactured; Davidson modifies with spirit-level horizon. 1866 ÔÇö Davidson publishes description in Journal of the Franklin Institute. 1867 ÔÇö Exhibited in Paris, Exposition Universelle. 1867ÔÇô1880s ÔÇö Active Coast Survey use in western U.S. and Alaska. Early 20th century ÔÇö Shipped by Davidson to Franklin D. Roosevelt at the Naval Observatory, Washington D.C. Later ÔÇö Preserved with original sextant case and associated dipleidoscope. Bibliography Davidson, George. ÔÇťSextant with Attached Spirit-Level Horizon.ÔÇŁ Journal of the Franklin Institute, 1866. Paris Universal Exposition, 1867. Reports of the United States Commissioners (Washington, 1869). Catalogue g├ęn├ęral, Exposition universelle de 1867, Paris, Group 2, Class 12. Jury Reports, Group II, Instruments of Precision (noting honorable mention).

metadata.label: Pub Title
metadata.value: Panorama vom Pizzo Centrale od. Tritton Sanct Gothard. (Blauberg der Dufourkarte), 3002 Meter ├╝ber Meer . Im Sommer 1868 aufgenommen u. auf Steingezeichnet von Albert Heim.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: ""This is a very fine view-point, and the panorama from it has been published by Prof. Albert Heim (Z├╝rich, 1867)" W.A.B. Coolidge, a great mountaineering pioneer, has written about Pizzo Centrale (in his guide "The Adula Alps" of 1893), which he had reached on July 29th, 1892, together with Christian Almer jun. It is the highest peak of the Gotthard range. Until the seventies it was a threethousander, measured at 3001 m. Due to a rock slide it lost parts of the summit and with them the triangular point. The new point was measured at 2999 m. Despite the lower altitude the summit has not lost its importance for tourists. Hikers enjoy a 360┬░ view from the mountain: including three valleys, a small lake to the south of the summit, a formerly vast glacier on the north side and to numerous mountain ranges in the distance." (Swiss Alpine Club, 2022)

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: Log Of H.M.S. "Swiftshure" Bearing the (flag of) Rear Admiral H.M.L. Lyons commanded by Captain C. Mainwaring, C.F. Henderson (with) Log of H.M.S. "Swiftshure" Bearing the (flag) of Rear Admiral J.K.E. Baird commanded by Captain the Honble T.S. Brand kept by C.F. Henderson

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: This is a ship's log that begins to record a journey midway from Honolulu to Esquimalt in British Columbia, then down the west coast of the United States and the west coast of South America, around Cape Horn, and back to England. It begins in July, 1884 and ends in November, 1885. 16 beautifully drawn charts record the track of the steam and sail powered ship, showing stops in San Francisco, Mazatlan, San Blas, Valparaiso and Rio de Janeiro and other ports. Many drawings illustrate aspects of the ship and its equipment. The log itself records the weather and daily ship operations. An unusual highly illustrated document.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

items.label: Yaggy's Geographical Portfolio
items.attribution: Property rights reside with the repository, Copyright (c) Stanford University.  Images may be reproduced or transmitted, but not for commercial use. For commercial use or commercial republication, contact rumseymapcenter@stanford.edu This work is licensed under a Creative Commons License. By downloading any images from this site, you agree to the terms of that license.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Yaggy's large geographical charts are among the most beautiful and impressive educational charts published in the 19th century. Using chromolithography, Yaggy created stunning colors and contrasts in his images of the world, its regions, and peoples. The ten charts in this set were originally housed in a large wooden portfolio that also served as a display mechanism, allowing the charts to be tilted up for classroom use. In the portfolio were also two elaborate charts of the universe with moving parts and tinted gels. In 1887 Yaggy published a set of charts with a similar theme, "Geographical Study: Comprising Physical, Political, Geological and Astronomical Geography," although it did not include any of the chromolithographic charts in our 1893 set. Rather it had more conventional maps of the world and nations. Yaggy intended his charts to be used in classrooms by students, and issued a teacherÔÇÖs handbook to assist with instruction. Yaggy published other educational works: Museum of Antiquity; A Description of Ancient LifeÔÇŽ (1880); The Royal Path of Life; Or, Aims and Aids to Success and Happiness (1881); The Standard History of All Nations and RacesÔÇŽ (1899); and How to Do a Consulting Library for Every Want (1902); YaggyÔÇÖs Anatomical Study; Presenting the Skeleton, Muscles, Arteries, Veins and Nerves, also Four Life-Size Manikins of the Body, the Head, the Eye and the Ear (Chicago, ca. 1884-1885), which featured life-size illustrations and cutaways of the human body.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: Lo sviluppo dell'industria italiana, by the Confederazione generale dell'industria italiana; published in Rome, 1929. National and thematic atlas of Italy, featuring the development of Italian industry. Bound in gray paper covers tied with maroon cord. Front cover includes title and imprint. Collation: [10] pages, LXXXIII plates. Volume includes a title page, table of contents and 3 maps and 109 charts on 83 plates. Maps show the industrial activity in the different regions of Italy. Charts represent various Italian industries - such as energy, manufacturing and agriculture - as well as their growth over time, from 1876 to 1928. "An absolutely delightful book, which is essentially an Italian take on the isotype, with the book having been largely composed of charts and diagrams, composed in the same manner as isotypes, but with a distinctly Italian twist. A rare book with OCLC listing no copies in the US and 2 in Europe." (Ursus, 2022)

metadata.label: Pub Reference
metadata.value: Senkevitch, Soviet architecture, 1917-1967 (Charlottesville, 1974), 205. Centre Pompidou, Paris-Moscou, 1900-1930 (Paris, 1979), 567. Andel, 301-302.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: APXđśTEKTđúPHđźIE đĄAHTAđŚđśđś, by Chernikhov, published in Leningrad by Meshdunarodnaja Kniga, 1933. Title translates to: Architectural fictions. Bound in board, covered in original blue cloth. Title debossed on both front cover and spine. Collation: [1-13], 14-102, 101 leaves of plates. Volume includes four parallel title pages, beginning with Russian and followed by translations in German, French and English, as well as a colophon in Russian. Table of contents for text lists section titles with associated page numbers. Section titles translate to: I. Types of architectural design -- II. Technical processes -- III. Imaging methods -- IV. Composite processes -- V. Techniques and display types -- VI. Methods for detecting architectural designs -- VII. Basic principles of building architectural fictions. Table of contents for plates lists titles of illustrations with associated plate numbers. Text illustrated by miniature images of architecture in black and white. Plates feature views of architectural fictions, in color and accompanied by descriptive text. Besides title page translations, all other text appears in Russian. ÔÇťFirst edition of the landmark work of Soviet architectural fantasies, lavishly illustrated with dramatic designs of possible cities, factories and monumental buildings. This is the best and most important publication by Chernykov, and one of the most exciting books on architecture issued in the twentieth century. Published in collaboration with D. Kopanitzin and E. Pavlova, this first edition was limited to 3,000 copies.ÔÇŁ - Ursus Books & Gallery, New York.

metadata.value: MOCKBA PEKOHCTPđúđśPđúETCđ» : AđŤđČđĹOđť đöđśAđôPAMM, TOđčOCXEM đś đĄOTOđôPAđĄđśđÖ đčO PEKOHCTPđúKđŽđśđś đôOP. MOCKBđź. MOCKBA 1938 đ│.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: MOCKBA PEKOHCTPđúđśPđúETCđ» : AđŤđČđĹOđť đöđśAđôPAMM, TOđčOCXEM đś đĄOTOđôPAđĄđśđÖ đčO PEKOHCTPđúKđŽđśđś đôOP, by Vsesoi´Şáu´Şízny─ş nauchno-izdatel╩╣ski─ş institut izobrazitel╩╣no─ş statistiki (Soviet Union) [= All-union Institute of Pictorial Statistics of Soviet Construction and Economy]. Published by the đśHCTđśTđúT đśđŚOCTAT [= INTITUTE IZOSTAT) in Moscow, 1938. Title translates to: Moscow under reconstruction. Bound in board covered in blue cloth. Front cover has title debossed and an embossed vignette of a city scene. Back cover has text: đśHCTđśTđúT đśđŚOCTAT [= INTITUTE IZOSTAT). Endpapers offer a view of the city skyline along the waterfront. Volume is 260 pages and contains 73 maps, 37 illustrations, 110 photographs, 41 diagrams and 3 portraits, including of Stalin. Through this variety of imagery, the atlas depicts MoscowÔÇÖs reconstruction during the 1930s. Many images show scenes of Moscow both before and after reconstruction, illustrating the projected development over time. Some projections imagine as far ahead as 1945, with estimated growth of entities such as schools, canals and power plants. In addition to featured themes, maps also show city limits, railways, roads, metro-lines, bus-lines, canals, bodies of water, drainage, topography, vegetation and the outskirts of the city. Volume includes a title page, colophon, foreword and acknowledgments. Title page features the slogan, "đčPOđŤETAPđśđś BCEX CTPAH, COEđöđśHđ»đÖTECđČ!" [= Workers of the world, unite!], a rallying cry from The communist manifesto by Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels.

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: The United States as viewed by California (Very unofficial), distorted and drawn by Ernest Dudley Chase, Winchester, Mass. Copyright 1940, Ernest Dudley Chase

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Uncolored pictorial map (as issued, see our hand colored copy, presumably colored by an owner). Showing the United States from the point of view of Californian, with the Sun smiling on a disproportionately large California while the rest of the country is compressed into a largely undifferentiated spiderÔÇÖs web beneath dark rain clouds. Throughout the state, California is also depicted as a cornucopia of plenty, teaming with industry, agriculture, recreational activity, wild life and natural wonders. Shows landmarks such as Spanish Colonial missions, the Rose Bowl, the Bakersfield oil wells and the state capital building. An airplane in the sky bearing the slogan ÔÇťweÔÇÖll soon be there!ÔÇŁ approaches the state line along with a train, a bus and cars, while railroad cars carrying California produce head east. Chase created this highly distorted view of the United States as perceived by Californians. One-third the size of the rest of the country, the state is perpetually drenched by the sun. Relief shown pictorially. Signed lower left in pencil: Ernest Dudley Chase.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

canvases.label: Image 1

items.label: California.1968 David Schiller

canvases.label: California.1968 David Schiller

metadata.label: Note
metadata.value: Pictorial 3-D map of the state of California by artist David Schiller, with engraved decorative borders. Includes many 1960s famous venues, bars and popular places. Shows buildings, bridges, roads, rivers, lakes, forests and numerous landmarks, some landmarks are noted. John F. Johnson's signature printed in the lower right and Jefferson Airplane, created by Schiller originally for the band and rendered on many Fillmore Posters and for album cover flying over. Relief shown pictorially. Includes compass rose oriented with the north to upper left margin. David Schiller was one of the kings of rock art, producing album covers and posters for many 1960's bands and creating many incredible Fillmore posters for most of the great psychedelic rock bands of the 1960's.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value:
Gregory Staple coined the term ÔÇťtelegeographyÔÇŁ in 1990 to describe the unprecedented new reach and impact of the worldÔÇÖs electronic communications networks.┬áRather than presaging the "death of distance", telegeography held that the rise of digital networks reflected an explosion of place (e.g., myriad new dialing codes, URLs, email addresses) that was dividing traditional social groups and splintering physical geographies of space and time .

In 1993, Staple founded┬áTeleGeography, Inc. (TGI) to research and compile reliable country-by-country statistics on the flow of international telephone and data traffic.┬áTGI also began to map the┬áphysical infrastructure underlying these communications streams (subsea cables, landing stations, communication satellites, tele-houses).┬á

Building on work published by LondonÔÇÖs International Institute of Communications (IIC), where Staple worked in the late 1980ÔÇÖs, TGI issued┬áannual statistical reports with┬ácommentary┬ádetailing the volume of cross-border telecommunications traffic and network links between over 50 countries - see the reports archive at https://www2.telegeography.com/telegeography-report-archive

In 1996, TGI began to publish an┬áannual poster-size map┬á┬áof the worldÔÇÖs submarine telecommunication cables and landing stations - https://www.davidrumsey.com/luna/servlet/s/i6v93f 
 
Some maps also included the footprints of major satellite networks. TGI's┬á2025 global communications map┬ácovers the approximately 600 international subsea cable┬ásystems that make up the InternetÔÇÖs principal electronic backbone - https://www.davidrumsey.com/luna/servlet/detail/RUMSEY~8~1~369655~90137030:Submarine-Cable-Map-2025?qvq=q:telegeography;lc:RUMSEY~8~1&mi=37&trs=39 ┬á

This┬á2014 Medium post┬árecounts the history of TGIÔÇÖs first 25 years - https://gregstaple.medium.com/mapping-tomorrow-c340d7dcc03f

Map and image are copyright by Telegeography, Inc. www.telegeography.com  For copies of TeleGeography maps that are still in print, see https://shop.telegeography.com/collections/telecom-maps
